title: Generics в рантайме: специализация и код-шаринг

cards.prompt: .NET reifies generics (unlike Java type erasure): typeof(T) inside a generic yields the real argument type at run time, so TN<int>/TN<string> print Int32/String and GetGenericArguments on List<int> also yields Int32
cards.code: using System; using System.Collections.Generic; static string TN<T>() => typeof(T).Name; Console.WriteLine($"{TN<int>()} {TN<string>()} {typeof(List<int>).GetGenericArguments()[0].Name}");

cards.prompt: One definition List`1 but distinct constructed types: GetGenericTypeDefinition matches (True) while the closed types List<string> and List<object> are different runtime types (False)
cards.code: using System; using System.Collections.Generic; bool sameDef = typeof(List<string>).GetGenericTypeDefinition() == typeof(List<object>).GetGenericTypeDefinition(); bool sameClosed = typeof(List<string>) == typeof(List<object>); Console.WriteLine($"{sameDef} {sameClosed}");

cards.prompt: List<object> boxes each int (reference-type layout) allocating 32056 bytes, while the value-specialized List<int> stores ints inline for 4056 bytes; same Add code, two runtime specializations of List`1
cards.code: using System; using System.Collections.Generic; const int N=1000; var w1=new List<object>(N); for(int i=0;i<N;i++)w1.Add(i); var w2=new List<int>(N); for(int i=0;i<N;i++)w2.Add(i); long a0=GC.GetAllocatedBytesForCurrentThread(); var boxed=new List<object>(N); for(int i=0;i<N;i++)boxed.Add(i); long a1=GC.GetAllocatedBytesForCurrentThread(); var inline=new List<int>(N); for(int i=0;i<N;i++)inline.Add(i); long a2=GC.GetAllocatedBytesForCurrentThread(); Console.WriteLine($"List<object>: {a1-a0} List<int>: {a2-a1}");
